Bone marrow-targeted RIT prior to allogeneic HSCT. Phase I in pediatric relapsed/refractory leukemia. BW250/183 is murine IgG1 originally developed as anti-CEA. | WEB ENRICHMENT: No relevant information found in the provided PubMed abstracts. The abstracts discuss: (1) FAP-RADS imaging for lung/urothelial cancer, (2) PD-L1 imaging with Cu-64/NOTA tracer, (3) hypoxia imaging with F-18 tracers, (4) FDG-PET for follicular lymphoma, and (5) thyroid cancer management survey. None contain information about Y-90-anti-CD66 (BW250/183 antibody), CD66/CEACAM targeting, or chelators used with this specific construct. The missing chelator information cannot be extracted from these articles. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Chelator confirmed as DTPA from peer-reviewed publications in Journal of Nuclear Medicine (2010) and PMC article (2015). Multiple sources describe the radiolabeling protocol using mx-DTPA (2-(p-SCN-Bz-)-6-methyl-diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid) as the bifunctional chelator for both In-111 and Y-90 labeling of BW250/183 anti-CD66 antibody.

Novel Lu-177 anti-B7-H3 mAb. B7-H3 is widely expressed across solid tumors. Phase 1/2a dose escalation across 10 tumor types. Radiopharm Theranostics is an Australian theranostics company. Chelator not specified in trial text. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Extensive web search conducted including company press releases, partnership announcements, IND approval news, and academic literature on Lu-177 antibody chelators. Multiple sources confirm 177Lu-BetaBart is manufactured by AtomVie with qualified radiolabeling process, but no public source discloses the specific chelator used (DOTA, CHX-A″-DTPA, AAZTA5, or other). This technical detail appears to remain proprietary at this stage of clinical development. Common chelators for therapeutic Lu-177 antibodies include DOTA (requiring heating) and CHX-A″-DTPA or AAZTA5 (allowing room temperature labeling), but without explicit confirmation for BetaBart, the chelator field cannot be reliably filled.

This is a diagnostic immunoPET imaging study using Zr-89-labeled atezolizumab to visualize PD-L1 expression in renal cell carcinoma. The trial explicitly states it is 'nontherapeutic' and aims to correlate imaging with PD-L1 expression and response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. Atezolizumab is an anti-PD-L1 monoclonal antibody (IgG1) developed by Genentech/Roche. DFO (desferrioxamine) is the standard chelator for Zr-89. The study involves two cohorts: localized RCC patients pre-nephrectomy and metastatic RCC patients pre-immunotherapy. This is pure diagnostic imaging, not radioimmunotherapy.

This is a diagnostic immunoPET imaging trial, not therapeutic RIT. Two PET tracers are being evaluated: [18F]F-AraG (a small molecule targeting activated T cells via mitochondrial deoxyguanosine kinase) and [89Zr]Zr-Df-Crefmirlimab (radiolabeled antibody). Only the second tracer qualifies as an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. Crefmirlimab (IAB22M2C) is an anti-PD-L1 monoclonal antibody developed by ImaginAb for immuno-PET imaging. The Df notation indicates deferoxamine (DFO) chelator. This is purely diagnostic imaging to assess T cell changes in relation to immunotherapy response in NSCLC patients. The [18F]F-AraG component is not antibody-based and therefore not extracted as a separate RIT asset.

This is a first-in-human immunoPET imaging study to detect CD46-positive prostate cancer. Zr-89 is a diagnostic radioisotope used for PET imaging with a ~78-hour half-life, suitable for antibody imaging. The study explicitly describes this as an immunoPET agent, not a therapeutic. DFO (desferrioxamine) is the standard chelator for Zr-89. The trial mentions a companion therapeutic antibody-drug conjugate (FOR46) under development by the same company, suggesting potential future theranostic development. YS5 appears to be the antibody name. Cold antibody dose optimization (Cohort B) is being performed to improve image quality. Fortis Therapeutics is listed as a collaborator and likely owns the asset, as they are developing the related FOR46 ADC. Antibody format inferred as full-length IgG based on typical immunoPET agents and dosing (20-50 mg), though not explicitly stated.
Conditions: Prostate Cancer, Metastatic Castration-resistant Prostate Cancer

Notes: This trial evaluates three different PET imaging agents for renal cell carcinoma: [68Ga]Ga-PSMA (small molecule, not antibody-based), [68Ga]Ga-DOTA-CAIX peptide (peptide, not antibody-based), and [68Ga]Ga-NOTA-CD70 antibody fragment (antibody-based). The specific antibody fragment format (Fab, scFv, nanobody, etc.) is not specified. This is an academic-sponsored trial; commercial rights status unknown. | WEB ENRICHMENT: None of the PubMed abstracts discuss CD70-targeted antibody fragments or the specific [68Ga]Ga-NOTA-CD70 antibody fragment asset from this trial. Article 1 and 5 discuss GPC3-targeted XH-06, Article 2 discusses CEA-targeted CE-21, Article 3 discusses HER2-targeting RAD202, and Article 4 discusses PD-L1-targeting sdAbs. Without any articles mentioning CD70 as the target or discussing the specific construct used in NCT07301827, the antibody name cannot be reliably extracted. No updates can be made. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Web search identified CD70-targeting single-domain antibodies RCCB3 and RCCB6 developed for 68Ga-NOTA labeling in clear cell renal cell carcinoma imaging. However, no specific evidence was found linking NCT07301827 at Union Hospital to any particular named antibody clone. The trial asset [68Ga]Ga-NOTA-CD70 antibody fragment appears to be an independently developed tracer at Union Hospital, but the specific antibody name/clone designation could not be determined from available web sources.

Notes: This is a diagnostic PET imaging study using a CD38-targeted nanobody labeled with Ga-68. The nanobody NB381 was developed by Lei Kang from Peking University First Hospital and Bing Jia's team. TOHP is used as the bifunctional chelator. The trial description mentions that this is a 'diagnostic and therapeutic agent' but the current study (Phase Early Phase 1) is focused solely on diagnostic PET imaging for multiple myeloma. CD38 is significantly overexpressed on malignant plasma cells in MM. Radiochemical purity >98%, stability not less than 90%. The probe shows high specificity for CD38. The intervention explicitly states this is an institutional intellectual property. This is a classic example of immunoPET using a nanobody scaffold rather than full-length antibody.

Notes: This is a diagnostic immunoPET study using Zr-89 to assess biodistribution and tumor uptake of the bispecific antibody S095012 (PD-L1x4-1BB). This is a pretargeted immunoPET imaging study. TF2 is a bispecific antibody targeting CEA (on tumor) and HSG (hapten). The radiolabeled peptide IMP-288 binds to the anti-HSG arm of TF2. The two-step pretargeting approach (antibody first, then radiolabeled peptide 30h later) allows tumor accumulation while clearing unbound antibody. HSG likely refers to histamine-succinyl-glycine or a similar hapten system. Ga-68 is a diagnostic PET isotope. This is purely diagnostic imaging to identify metastases suitable for surgical resection or local ablation in colorectal cancer patients. The chelator for Ga-68-IMP-288 is not specified but is likely DOTA or NOTA based on standard Ga-68 chemistry. IMP-288 is the radiolabeled peptide component, not the antibody itself. | WEB ENRICHMENT: Chelator confirmed as DOTA from PMID:28232604 which explicitly describes 'the di-HSG-DOTA peptide IMP288' used with the same bispecific antibody TF2 for pretargeted radioimmunotherapy. This matches the trial's construct (TF2/68Ga-IMP-288) and pretargeting approach.

The asset appears to be a closely held proprietary development with technical specifications not yet publicly disclosed in press releases, regulatory filings, conference abstracts, or patent literature.

False positive: 177Lu-PSMA-617 is a small-molecule radioligand (peptide-based), not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. PSMA-617 uses a small molecule ligand to target PSMA, not a monoclonal antibody. This is similar to Pluvicto/Lutathera class of compounds. The trial combines this radioligand with immune checkpoint inhibitors (ipilimumab and nivolumab) but the radiopharmaceutical component itself is not antibody-based. Diagnostic companion imaging uses 68Ga-PSMA PET, also a small molecule radioligand.
Lead_Sponsor: Australian and New Zealand Urogenital and Prostate Cancer Trials Group
Conditions: Prostatic Neoplasms, Genital Neoplasms, Male, Urogenital Neoplasms, Prostate Cancer, Neoplasms by Site, Neoplasms, Prostatic Disease

Notes: Theranostic approach using In-111-DOTA-girentuximab for imaging/dosimetry (5 mCi) followed by Lu-177-DOTA-girentuximab for therapy (65 mCi/m2). Girentuximab is also known as cG250, a chimeric monoclonal antibody targeting carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX), which is highly expressed in clear cell renal cell carcinoma. HACA monitoring indicates chimeric antibody (mouse-human). The study uses pre-therapeutic imaging to confirm tumor targeting before administering therapeutic dose. Up to 3 treatment cycles permitted at 75% dose reduction per cycle. Radboud University Medical Center appears to be the academic sponsor; commercial rights to girentuximab may be with industry partners.
Conditions: Metastatic Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma

Notes: 177Lu-girentuximab is Telix's TLX250-CDx adapted for therapeutic use. Girentuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX), which is overexpressed in clear cell RCC. This is a combination trial with cabozantinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) and nivolumab (checkpoint inhibitor). The trial also includes [18F]F-AraG imaging agent for exploratory endpoints. Telix is listed as collaborator, confirming asset ownership. Standard DOTA chelation is typical for Lu-177 conjugates.
Conditions: Advanced Cancer, Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma

Notes: FALSE POSITIVE: [177Lu]Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) is a small-molecule peptide radioligand, NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. DOTATATE is an octreotide analog (somatostatin analog peptide) that targets somatostatin receptor 2 (SSTR2). This is peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT), not radioimmunotherapy (RIT). The trial mentions SSTR2 targeting and uses standard DOTATATE nomenclature. This is a well-known commercial product (Novartis Lutathera) approved for neuroendocrine tumors, now being tested in meningioma. Does not meet RIT criteria as it lacks an antibody component.

False positive: This is NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. [177Lu]Lu-PSMA-617 is a small-molecule radioligand (peptide-based PSMA inhibitor), not a radiolabeled antibody. PSMA-617 is a DOTA-conjugated small molecule that binds to PSMA receptors. This is the same class as Pluvicto. The trial combines this small-molecule radioligand with ipilimumab (an unconjugated antibody checkpoint inhibitor), but ipilimumab is not radiolabeled. Neither component meets the RIT definition of a radiolabeled antibody or antibody-like protein.

Theranostic pair: [111In]-FPI-1547 for imaging (patient selection) and [225Ac]-FPI-1434 for therapy. Both use FPI-1175 antibody targeting IGF-1R. The study includes cold antibody (FPI-1175) dosing strategy to potentially optimize biodistribution. Ac-225 is an alpha-emitting radionuclide. The chelator is described as 'bifunctional chelate' but specific type (DOTA, DTPA, macropa, etc.) is not stated. Humanized monoclonal antibody format indicates full-length IgG. This is a first-in-human study for this alpha-emitting RIT agent.
Conditions: Advanced Solid Tumours, Endometrial Cancer, Cervical Cancer, Ovarian Cancer, Breast Cancer, Triple Negative Breast Cancer (TNBC), HER2-negative Breast Cancer, Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma (HNSCC), Adrenocortical Carcinoma, Uveal Melanoma

Notes: This is a combination trial with lintuzumab-Ac-225 (RIT component) plus venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor) and ASTX-727 (decitabine/cedazuridine hypomethylating agent). Lintuzumab is a humanized anti-CD33 monoclonal antibody conjugated to Ac-225, a therapeutic alpha-emitting radioisotope. CD33 is expressed on AML blasts. The trial description explicitly states the antibody 'attaches to CD33 positive cancer cells in a targeted way and delivers actinium Ac 225 to kill them.' The specific chelator is not mentioned in the trial description but is likely a bifunctional chelator suitable for Ac-225 (such as DOTA or macropa derivatives). Asset owner is likely Actinium Pharmaceuticals, which has developed lintuzumab-based constructs, though this is an NCI-sponsored trial. Two administration schedules are being compared (day 1 vs day 8 dosing). | WEB ENRICHMENT: Multiple abstracts describe lintuzumab conjugated to Ac-225, but none explicitly mention the chelator used. PMID:35247915 describes '225Ac-lintuzumab' as 'lintuzumab conjugated to actinium-225' without specifying chelator chemistry. PMID:39955432 describes it as 'humanized anti-CD33 antibody linked to Actinium-225' but provides no chelator details. Given that all papers clearly describe the same asset (225Ac-lintuzumab/Actimab-A) used in clinical trials with direct NCT references and consistent formulation, and none mention the chelator despite detailed pharmacological descriptions, it appears the chelator information was not disclosed in these publications. The field should remain as 'Not disclosed' rather than being filled with a value. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Chelator confirmed as DOTA (p-SCN-Bn-DOTA/DOTA-SCN) from multiple peer-reviewed publications and conference abstracts. PMC9106874 states '225Ac-lintuzumab is a radioimmunoconjugate composed of humanized anti-CD33 monoclonal antibody lintuzumab linked to 225Ac, via the bifunctional chelate DOTA-SCN.' ASH 2023 abstract confirms 'Lintuzumab-Ac225 was generated by conjugating lintuzumab with p-SCN-Bn-DOTA.' PMC7897952 also confirms use of p-SCN-Bn-DOTA for conjugation.

False positive. This trial involves cetuximab (an unconjugated monoclonal antibody against EGFR) combined with ZEN003694 (a BET bromodomain inhibitor) and encorafenib (a BRAF inhibitor). None of these agents are radiolabeled. Cetuximab is used as immunotherapy, not as a radiopharmaceutical. No antibody-based radiopharmaceutical is involved in this trial.
Conditions: Metastatic Colorectal Adenocarcinoma, Recurrent Colorectal Adenocarcinoma, Refractory Colorectal Adenocarcinoma, Stage IV Colorectal Cancer AJCC v8

Notes: This is a theranostic pair: [225Ac]-FPI-2068 for therapy (alpha emitter actinium-225) and [111In]-FPI-2107 for imaging/dosimetry (indium-111 SPECT). Both use the same bispecific antibody FPI-2053 targeting EGFR and cMET. The study describes FPI-2053 as a 'directed bispecific monovalent antibody' targeting two receptors commonly overexpressed in solid tumors. The specific chelator is described as 'bifunctional chelating agent' but the exact chemistry is not specified (could be DOTA, DTPA, macropa, or other). The theranostic approach allows for patient-specific dosimetry using In-111 imaging before Ac-225 therapy. Part A optimizes the cold antibody pre-dose, Part B escalates the Ac-225 dose. This represents a novel targeted alpha therapy (TAT) platform.
Conditions: Advanced Solid Tumor, Metastatic Colorectal Carcinoma, Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Non-small Cell Lung Cancer, Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma, Gastric Cancer, Renal Cell Carcinoma

Notes: Alpha-particle emitter (Ac-225) for targeted therapy. M5A is a humanized anti-CEA monoclonal antibody. CEA is a well-established tumor marker for colorectal and other cancers. Phase I dose-escalation study to establish MTD. City of Hope appears to be both sponsor and developer, with NCI collaboration.

False positive - not RIT. This trial combines cetuximab (unconjugated monoclonal antibody targeting EGFR) with recombinant interleukin-12 (immune stimulant). No radiolabeling is involved. Cetuximab is administered IV as a standard unconjugated antibody therapy, while IL-12 is given SC as a separate immunomodulatory agent. This is an immunotherapy combination, not a radioimmunotherapy.
Conditions: Metastatic Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Recurrent Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Unresectable Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma

Notes: Actinium-225-labeled antibody targeting human kallikrein-2 (hK2), a prostate-specific biomarker. hK2 is a serine protease related to PSA and expressed in prostate tissue. This is an alpha-emitting RIT targeting advanced prostate cancer. Trial includes combination with JNJ-78278343 (likely another agent, possibly checkpoint inhibitor or androgen receptor pathway inhibitor) and stereotactic body radiation therapy in Part 3. Janssen is the clear asset owner. Chelator likely DOTA or similar bifunctional chelator suitable for Ac-225, but not explicitly stated. Antibody format presumed full-length IgG based on typical development for this class of therapeutics, though not explicitly confirmed. | WEB ENRICHMENT: The provided PubMed article (PMID:41531507) discusses MDPK67b, a recombinant protease inhibitor targeting KLK2 and other kallikreins in prostate cancer. This is unrelated to NCT04644770, which involves JNJ-69086420, an Ac-225 labeled antibody targeting hK2 (human kallikrein-2). While both involve hK2/KLK2 as a target, the article describes a protease inhibitor therapy mechanism rather than radioimmunotherapy with an antibody construct. No information about chelators, linker technology, or antibody names for JNJ-69086420 could be extracted from this abstract. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Chelator confirmed as DOTA from multiple authoritative sources including NCI Cancer Drug Dictionary, ResearchGate publication, and ASCO GU 2022 abstract. The asset is consistently described as 225Ac-DOTA-h11B6 (JNJ-69086420), where DOTA refers to 1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetraacetic acid. Linker technology inferred as direct conjugation since no specialized site-specific conjugation methods, pretargeting systems, or alternative linker technologies were mentioned in any clinical trial descriptions or conference abstracts.

This trial compares amivantamab (a bispecific monoclonal antibody targeting EGFR and MET) with cetuximab (anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody) for cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma in immunocompromised patients. Neither intervention involves radiolabeling. The hyaluronidase component is used to facilitate subcutaneous delivery of amivantamab, not as a radiopharmaceutical component. This is NOT a radioimmunotherapy trial - it involves unconjugated monoclonal antibodies without any radioisotopes. False positive pattern: Unconjugated antibodies in oncology context.
Conditions: Locally Recurrent Skin Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Metastatic Skin Squamous Cell Carcinoma

Notes: This is a therapeutic alpha-emitter RIT targeting CD33 on myeloid leukemia cells. HuM195 is a humanized monoclonal antibody developed by Memorial Sloan Kettering. Actinium Pharmaceuticals is listed as a collaborator and likely holds commercial rights. The trial also mentions 213Bi-HuM195 combination with cytarabine, indicating prior work with bismuth-213. Ac-225 is an alpha-emitter ('Targeted Atomic Nano-Generators' refers to alpha particles). Chelator not specified but likely DOTA or similar bifunctional chelator suitable for actinium. Starting dose 0.5 μCi/kg with dose escalation planned. | WEB ENRICHMENT: PMID:14734685 directly describes 225Ac-HuM195 construct used in cynomolgus monkey toxicity studies, referring to it as a 'targetable atomic generator' with Ac-225 conjugated to HuM195 antibody. The paper describes the construct as '225Ac nanogenerators' where monoclonal antibody HuM195 is 'the carrier' for Ac-225, and discusses the alpha-emitting daughter elements that are 'no longer bound to the carrier IgG' after generation. This indicates direct conjugation methodology typical of actinium-225 antibody constructs. However, the specific chelator (likely DOTA or DTPA for Ac-225) is not explicitly named in the abstract. The linker technology is inferred as direct conjugation based on the description of Ac-225 being conjugated to the IgG carrier, though site-specific vs. random conjugation is not specified. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Chelator identified as DOTA (1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetraacetic acid) from multiple peer-reviewed publications and patent filings. The specific construct is [Ac-225]-p-SCN-Bn-DOTA/HuM195. DOTA was established as the standard chelator for 225Ac-HuM195 in both preclinical studies and human clinical trials for advanced myeloid leukemias.

First-in-human alpha-emitter RIT targeting GPC3 in hepatocellular carcinoma. Actinium-225 is a therapeutic alpha-emitter with short tissue penetration range. Study includes non-radioactive preinjection (BAY 3547922) likely for receptor saturation/blocking strategy. GPC3 (Glypican-3) is a validated target overexpressed in HCC. Antibody format inferred from 'antibody conjugate' terminology and typical format for targeted radiopharmaceuticals. Chelator not specified but Ac-225 typically uses DOTA or similar macrocyclic chelators. Three-part study design: dose escalation (Part 1), expansion monotherapy (Part 2), and combination therapy (Part 3). | WEB ENRICHMENT: Abstract explicitly describes BAY 3547926 as an '225Ac-labeled antibody-chelator conjugate (ACC)' that delivers 225Ac to GPC3-expressing cells, confirming the direct conjugation approach. However, the specific chelator chemistry (e.g., DOTA, DTPA, macropa) is not disclosed in the abstract. Antibody_Name also not mentioned - only the construct name BAY 3547926 is provided. Since the abstract does not contain the missing specific information requested (chelator identity and antibody name), no updates can be provided. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Web search conducted across official Bayer press releases, AACR 2025 presentation summaries, and multiple news sources. All sources confirm BAY 3547926 is an Ac-225 labeled anti-GPC3 monoclonal antibody for HCC, but none disclose the specific chelator (DOTA vs DOTAGA vs DO3A) or the proprietary antibody name/clone. These technical details were not included in the April 2025 AACR presentation or any public disclosures. Chelator and antibody identity remain proprietary at this early stage.
Countries: Belgium, Canada, Finland, Spain, United Kingdom

Notes: This is a theranostic study using both therapeutic Ac-225-labeled vofatamab ([225Ac]-FPI-1966) and diagnostic In-111-labeled vofatamab ([111In]-FPI-1967) for imaging/biodistribution. Vofatamab is described as a human monoclonal antibody targeting FGFR3. The specific chelator is not named but described as a 'bifunctional chelate'. Actinium-225 is an alpha-emitting radionuclide used for targeted alpha therapy. The study includes pre-dosing with unlabeled vofatamab to potentially optimize biodistribution. Phase 1/2 first-in-human study for advanced FGFR3-expressing solid tumors including bladder, head and neck, and other cancers.
Conditions: Advanced Solid Tumor, Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Bladder Carcinoma, Susceptible FGFR3 Genetic Alterations, FGFR3, FGFR3 Overexpression, FGFR3 Receptor, FGFR3 Protein Overexpression, Ovarian Cancer, Colorectal Cancer, Breast Cancer, Liver Cancer, Lung Cancer, Gastric Cancer

Notes: False positive: This trial involves Y-90 radioembolization (TARE) using glass microspheres (TheraSphere), which is a locoregional brachytherapy technique, NOT antibody-based radioimmunotherapy. The Y-90 is embedded in glass microspheres for direct arterial delivery to the liver tumor vasculature. The trial also includes durvalumab (anti-PD-L1 antibody) and bevacizumab (anti-VEGF antibody), but these are unconjugated therapeutic antibodies used in combination with the radioembolization procedure - they are not radiolabeled. This is a combination therapy approach (locoregional radiation + immunotherapy + anti-angiogenic therapy), not RIT.

False positive - NOT RIT. This trial uses SIR-Spheres Yttrium-90 microspheres for selective internal radiation therapy (SIRT/radioembolization), which are radioactive microspheres delivered directly to liver tumors via hepatic artery, NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. The immunotherapy component (ipilimumab and nivolumab) consists of unconjugated checkpoint inhibitor antibodies given separately, not radiolabeled. This is a combination study of radioembolization + immunotherapy, not radioimmunotherapy.

False positive. This trial involves Y-90 radioembolization (mentioned in keywords), but this is a liver-directed therapy using radioactive microspheres, not a radiolabeled antibody. The trial interventions are durvalumab (anti-PD-L1 antibody, not radiolabeled) and SNDX-6352 (CSF-1R inhibitor, not radiolabeled). The Y-90 radioembolization is administered prior to the immunotherapy combination as a separate preparatory treatment. This matches the false positive pattern of external/separate radiation therapy combined with unconjugated antibodies.

Notes: This is NOT an RIT trial. This is a combination therapy trial using: (1) SIR-Spheres (Y-90 resin microspheres) - a non-antibody radioembolization therapy for liver metastases delivered via hepatic artery, and (2) systemic chemotherapy with cetuximab (unconjugated anti-EGFR antibody) plus irinotecan. The Y-90 microspheres are NOT conjugated to an antibody - they are resin beads containing Y-90 that lodge in liver tumor vasculature. Cetuximab is given separately as a standard unconjugated therapeutic antibody. This matches the false positive pattern of 'External beam radiation + antibody combination' where the radioactive component and antibody are separate treatments, not a conjugated radiopharmaceutical.

This is NOT an RIT trial. It is a combination trial of SIRT (Selective Internal Radiation Therapy) using Y-90 microspheres (SIR-Spheres) followed by nivolumab (anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor antibody). The Y-90 is delivered via resin microspheres in a radioembolization procedure, not conjugated to an antibody. Nivolumab is an unconjugated therapeutic antibody used for immunotherapy, not radiolabeled. This represents a combination of two separate modalities: locoregional radiotherapy (Y-90 microspheres) and systemic immunotherapy (nivolumab). Neither component is an antibody-radiopharmaceutical conjugate. Classification: False positive - combination of separate radiation therapy and unconjugated antibody.
Lead_Sponsor: Clinica Universidad de Navarra, Universidad de Navarra

Notes: False positive: Therasphere (Y-90) is a radioembolization device using yttrium-90 microspheres for liver-directed therapy, NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. The Y-90 is incorporated into glass or resin microspheres that are delivered via hepatic artery embolization. This trial combines Y-90 radioembolization with subsequent systemic therapy (immunotherapy or TKIs), but neither component involves radiolabeled antibodies. The atezolizumab and bevacizumab are unconjugated antibodies given separately from the radiation treatment.

hPAM4 is a humanized anti-MUC1 monoclonal antibody developed by Immunomedics (now part of Gilead Sciences). The trial keyword explicitly states 'MUC1 antibody'. This is a combination trial with gemcitabine chemotherapy in pancreatic cancer. The chelator and linker technology are not specified in the trial description but DTPA or DOTA would be typical for Y-90 labeling. The intervention is clearly therapeutic given the fractionated dosing schedule and combination with standard chemotherapy. | WEB ENRICHMENT: No relevant information found in the provided abstracts. Article 1 (PMID:22406590) mentions 90Y-hPAM4 but only describes it as 'yttrium labeled anti-mucin humanized antibody' without specifying the chelator or linker technology. Articles 2-5 discuss unrelated radiopharmaceuticals (90Y-FAPI-46, 177Lu-PSMA-617, 177Lu-DOTATATE) and do not contain information about the hPAM4 construct used in NCT00603863. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Chelator confirmed as DOTA (also called tetraxetan) from multiple clinical trial publications including JNM 2007 phase I abstract which explicitly names the compound as 90Y-DOTA-hPAM4. Linker technology identified as direct conjugation via lysine residues - ADC Review database indicates the DOTA chelator is conjugated to an average of 4-7 lysyl residues through their N6 amine groups via standard amide bond formation. This is the conventional direct conjugation method for antibody-chelator attachment.

False positive - not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. This trial combines two separate interventions: (1) Y-90 glass microspheres for radioembolization (selective internal radiation therapy/SIRT), which are radioactive glass beads delivered via hepatic artery to treat liver tumors through local radiation, not an antibody conjugate; and (2) ipilimumab, an unconjugated immune checkpoint inhibitor antibody (anti-CTLA-4) given systemically and separately. The Y-90 microspheres are not antibody-targeted and rely on size-based physical trapping in tumor vasculature. Ipilimumab is not radiolabeled. This is a combination therapy study, not RIT.

Clear RIT trial using radiolabeled anti-CD25 monoclonal antibody (basiliximab). Basiliximab is an FDA-approved chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting CD25 (IL-2 receptor alpha chain), originally approved for transplant rejection. This trial repurposes it as a radioimmunotherapeutic by conjugating Y-90 via DOTA chelator for T-cell NHL. The trial is part of a combination regimen (aTAC BEAM) with standard BEAM chemotherapy prior to autologous stem cell transplant. Sponsored by City of Hope Medical Center, an academic institution with NCI collaboration. The 90Y-basiliximab/DOTA construct appears to be an investigational academic asset.

False positive - not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. These are separate therapeutic modalities used in combination, not a radioimmunotherapy.

This is NOT an RIT trial. This is a combination trial of nivolumab (an unconjugated immune checkpoint inhibitor antibody targeting PD-1) with Y-90 glass microspheres (radioembolization/SIRT). The Y-90 microspheres (Therasphere) are not antibody-based - they are radioactive glass beads delivered via hepatic artery for locoregional treatment of liver tumors. Nivolumab is given separately as an IV infusion and is not radiolabeled. This represents a false positive match: radiation therapy (radioembolization) combined with an unconjugated antibody, but not a radioimmunotherapeutic conjugate.
Conditions: Stage IIIA Hepatocellular Carcinoma, Stage IIIB Hepatocellular Carcinoma, Stage IIIC Hepatocellular Carcinoma, Stage IVA Hepatocellular Carcinoma, Stage IVB Hepatocellular Carcinoma

Notes: This is NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical (RIT). This trial involves two separate interventions: (1) Cemiplimab - an unconjugated monoclonal antibody (anti-PD-1 immunotherapy) given systemically, and (2) Y-90 resin microspheres (SIR-Spheres) - radioactive microspheres delivered via transarterial radioembolization (TARE) for local liver tumor treatment. The Y-90 is encapsulated in resin microspheres, not conjugated to an antibody. This is a combination trial of immunotherapy plus radioembolization, but the radioisotope is not antibody-targeted. Classic false positive pattern: immunotherapy antibody + radiation therapy are separate modalities, not a radioimmunoconjugate.
Conditions: Anatomic Stage IV Breast Cancer AJCC v8, Metastatic Breast Carcinoma, Metastatic Carcinoma in the Liver

Notes: FALSE POSITIVE: This trial uses SIR-Spheres, which are Y-90 microspheres for selective internal radiation therapy (SIRT) delivered directly to the liver via hepatic artery catheterization. This is NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. The Y-90 is embedded in resin or glass microspheres, not conjugated to an antibody. Cetuximab (anti-EGFR antibody) and erlotinib (EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor) are given as separate, unconjugated systemic therapies to enhance radiosensitization. This is a combination of radioembolization (Y-90 microspheres) plus EGFR-targeted therapy, not radioimmunotherapy.

FALSE POSITIVE: This trial uses Yttrium-90 microspheres for selective internal radiation therapy (SIRT), which is a radioembolization procedure where radioactive microspheres are delivered directly to liver tumors via hepatic artery. This is NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. The Y-90 is embedded in resin or glass microspheres (e.g., SIR-Spheres, TheraSphere), not conjugated to an antibody. This is a form of brachytherapy/radioembolization, not radioimmunotherapy. The trial also includes FOLFIRI chemotherapy and bevacizumab (unconjugated antibody), but these are separate interventions, not radiolabeled antibodies.

False positive - not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. This trial uses SIRT-Y90 (Selective Internal Radiation Therapy with Y-90 resin microspheres/SIR-Spheres), which are radioactive microspheres for liver-directed therapy, NOT a radiolabeled antibody. The Y-90 is embedded in resin microspheres that are delivered via hepatic artery to tumor vasculature. The trial combines this with unconjugated antibodies (atezolizumab anti-PD-L1 + bevacizumab anti-VEGF) administered separately as IV infusions, but these antibodies are not radiolabeled. This is a combination therapy trial, not radioimmunotherapy.

Asset_Name: Yttrium Y 90 anti-CD45 monoclonal antibody AHN-12
Notes: CD45 is a pan-leukocyte marker expressed on all hematopoietic cells except erythrocytes and platelets, making it a relevant target for leukemia. Trial includes biodistribution phase using In-111 labeled antibody for dosimetry followed by therapeutic Y-90 labeled antibody. Dose escalation study (2.5-12.5 mCi/m^2). Cold antibody pre-dose of 150 mg/m^2. Chelator not specified in intervention description but Y-90 typically requires DOTA or DTPA for conjugation to antibodies. | WEB ENRICHMENT: Chelator identified as 1B4M-DTPA from PMID:12804039, which explicitly describes labeling the AHN-12 antibody with Y-90 using this chelate. The abstract states 'The antibody was readily labeled with (90)Y using the highly stable chelate 1B4M-DTPA' for the same antibody (AHN-12) and isotope (Y-90) as the trial.
Lead_Sponsor: Masonic Cancer Center, University of Minnesota

Notes: False positive: This trial involves Y-90 TARE (Transarterial Radioembolization) which uses Y-90 microspheres (glass or resin beads), not antibody-based radiopharmaceuticals. TARE is a locoregional radiotherapy technique for liver tumors using particulate formulations. The trial combines Y-90 TARE with immunotherapy (atezolizumab, an anti-PD-L1 antibody) and bevacizumab (an anti-VEGF antibody), but neither antibody is radiolabeled. This is a combination therapy study, not RIT.

This is a classic therapeutic RIT trial for pancreatic cancer. PAM4 is a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting MUC1, a mucin glycoprotein overexpressed in pancreatic adenocarcinoma. The trial explicitly states 'humanized PAM4 IgG' confirming the antibody format. A companion diagnostic dose of 111In-hPAM4 is used for pre-therapy imaging, biodistribution, and dosimetry studies, which is standard practice in RIT trials. The chelator is not specified in the available information but would typically be DTPA or DOTA for Y-90 labeling. Gilead Sciences acquired Immunomedics (the original developer of hPAM4/clivatuzumab) assets. This is a dose-escalation phase I/II study to determine MTD and preliminary efficacy. | WEB ENRICHMENT: No relevant information found in the provided PubMed abstracts. Article 1 (PMID:22406590) mentions 90Y-hPAM4 but only describes it as 'yttrium labeled anti-mucin humanized antibody' without specifying the chelator or linker technology. The other articles discuss unrelated radiopharmaceuticals (90Y-FAPI-46, 177Lu-PSMA-617, 177Lu-DOTATATE) or completely unrelated topics (thromboxane signaling). Without explicit information about the chelator or conjugation method for hPAM4, no fields can be reliably extracted. | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Chelator confirmed as DOTA (tetraxetan) from multiple sources including Journal of Nuclear Medicine abstract (90Y-DOTA-hPAM4) and PMC clinical trial publication. Linker technology identified as direct conjugation to lysine residues (4-7 lysyl groups per antibody, linked via N6 amine) from ADC Review database and patent filing EP1689448B1. DOTA is conjugated to hPAM4 antibody using activated DOTA that reacts with lysine residues on the antibody surface.

This is a three-arm randomized phase III trial comparing CHOP alone (Arm I, closed), CHOP + rituximab (Arm II), and CHOP + I-131 tositumomab (Arm III, the RIT component) for newly diagnosed follicular NHL. Tositumomab is a murine IgG2a anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that is directly radioiodinated with I-131. This was marketed as Bexxar before being withdrawn from the market in 2014. The trial monitors for human anti-mouse antibody (HAMA) development. The RIT is administered after completion of CHOP chemotherapy on days 134 and 141 (dosimetric dose followed by therapeutic dose). Asset_Owner is GSK based on historical ownership of Bexxar, though this was developed by Corixa initially.

This is a Phase I dose-escalation trial of radioiodinated murine J591 antibody targeting PSMA in androgen-independent prostate cancer. The 'mu' prefix indicates murine origin (compared to humanized J591 used in later trials with Lu-177). I-131 is directly conjugated to the antibody via radioiodination, not via a chelator. The trial includes biodistribution, dosimetry, and safety assessment. PSMA is a well-validated target for prostate cancer. Memorial Sloan Kettering developed the J591 antibody series.

This is NOT a radioimmunotherapy trial. Although the keywords mention 'MAB 131 I - 3F8' (suggesting I-131 radiolabeled 3F8), the actual intervention described uses unconjugated 3F8 antibody in combination with allogeneic NK cells and chemotherapy. There is no mention of radiolabeling in the intervention description or protocol. The 3F8 antibody targets GD2 (a disialoganglioside on neuroblastoma cells) and is used here to enhance NK cell-mediated antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), not as a radiopharmaceutical. This is a cellular immunotherapy trial combined with unconjugated antibody therapy.
Conditions: Neuroblastoma, Bone Marrow, Sympathetic Nervous System

Notes: False positive. This trial compares radiofrequency ablation (a physical ablation technique) versus I-131 treatment for thyroid nodules. I-131 in this context is used as free radioactive iodine for thyroid ablation, not as an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. There is no antibody component - this is direct I-131 uptake by thyroid tissue due to physiological iodine metabolism. Not an RIT asset.
Conditions: Thyroid Nodule, Toxic or With Hyperthyroidism, Autonomous Thyroid Function, Thyroid Nodule; Hyperthyroidism, Radiofrequency Ablation, Iodine Hyperthyroidism, Iodine Adverse Reaction

Notes: This trial involves hu14.18-IL2, which is an antibody-interleukin fusion protein (immunocytokine), not a radioimmunotherapy agent. The hu14.18 antibody targets GD2 antigen on neuroblastoma cells and is fused to interleukin-2 to enhance immune activation. There is no radioisotope component. Although MIBG scanning is mentioned for disease evaluation, this is a diagnostic tool to measure disease response, not the therapeutic agent itself. This is an immunotherapy trial, not RIT.

False positive. This trial uses iodine-131 (radioactive iodine) for thyroid ablation in Graves' disease, not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. The study compares different timing strategies for levothyroxine (LT4) hormone replacement therapy after radioiodine ablation. I-131 is administered as sodium iodide for direct thyroid uptake, not conjugated to an antibody. This is standard endocrine treatment for hyperthyroidism, not radioimmunotherapy.

This is a diagnostic imaging study using I-131-labeled murine monoclonal antibody 8H9 for tumor detection. The study explicitly states the purpose is 'to find out whether the monoclonal antibody 8H9 is useful in finding tumors' and uses imaging doses (10 mCi/1.73m^2). Murine monoclonal antibody suggests full-length IgG format. The specific antigen target for 8H9 is not mentioned in the trial description. This is 8H9's first-in-human study. The antibody is administered with unlabeled 8H9 protein (50mg/1.73m^2). I-131 is directly labeled to the antibody (radioiodination). Conditions include CNS tumors, neuroblastoma, and sarcoma. MSKCC is the developer and sponsor. | WEB ENRICHMENT: Target antigen identified as B7-H3 from PMID:30879172 which describes '131I-Omburtamab targeting B7-H3' in ETMR treatment. Omburtamab is the international nonproprietary name (INN) for antibody 8H9. Confidence HIGH: Omburtamab/8H9 is a globally unique antibody name developed at Memorial Sloan Kettering.

False positive: This trial involves radioiodine (I-131) ablation therapy for Graves' disease/thyroid tissue remnants after thyroidectomy. Radioiodine directly accumulates in thyroid tissue without an antibody targeting component. This is standard nuclear medicine thyroid ablation, not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. The radioiodine uptake is mediated by the sodium-iodide symporter in thyroid cells, not by antibody targeting.

Cotara is a chimeric monoclonal antibody (chTNT-1/B) radiolabeled with I-131. TNT antibodies target DNA-histone complexes exposed in necrotic tumor tissue. The antibody is administered via direct interstitial infusion into the tumor cavity over 25 hours, delivering radiation to the tumor core. This is a dose-finding study (1.5-2.5 mCi/cc) to confirm MTD and characterize dosimetry. Peregrine Pharmaceuticals developed this asset for glioblastoma multiforme treatment. The targeting mechanism differs from most RIT as it targets necrotic tissue rather than surface antigens on live tumor cells.

This is a radioimmunotherapy trial using I-131-labeled anti-tenascin monoclonal antibody 81C6. Tenascin is an extracellular matrix protein overexpressed in gliomas. The antibody is administered intracystically (directly into brain tumor cysts), which is an unusual route but appropriate for cystic brain lesions. 81C6 is a well-characterized murine monoclonal antibody developed at Duke University. Radioiodination is the standard conjugation method for iodine isotopes. Phase I dose-escalation study to assess toxicity and preliminary efficacy in recurrent cystic gliomas.
Conditions: Brain and Central Nervous System Tumors

Notes: False positive: Not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. This is an observational study investigating endogenous autoantibodies in POTS patients. The I-131 tagged human serum albumin (25 micro-Ci) is used as a tracer for blood volume measurement, not as a therapeutic or diagnostic antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. No radiolabeled antibody or antibody-like protein is administered. Study focus is on characterizing naturally occurring autoantibodies against adrenergic receptors, not testing an RIT asset.
Conditions: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome, Postural Tachycardia Syndrome, Tachycardia, Arrhythmias, Cardiac, Autonomic Nervous System Diseases, Orthostatic Intolerance, Cardiovascular Diseases, Primary Dysautonomias

Notes: BIBH-1 is a monoclonal antibody F19 targeting fibroblast activation protein (FAP), which is expressed in tumor stroma. The 'BIBH' prefix suggests Boehringer Ingelheim as the developer. I-131 labeling is used for both therapeutic effect and biodistribution/imaging studies. The trial design uses I-131 labeling on weeks 1, 5, and 9 of 12-week treatment cycles. FAP is a target on cancer-associated fibroblasts, not tumor cells directly, making this an interesting stroma-targeting approach. Academic sponsor (Memorial Sloan Kettering) testing an industry asset. Full-length IgG format inferred from 'monoclonal antibody' terminology and 60-minute infusion time.

This is a Phase I imaging and pharmacokinetics study in patients scheduled for surgical resection. The primary objectives focus on toxicity assessment, pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, and imaging characteristics. Surgery occurs ~7 days post-infusion, suggesting the I-131 labeling is used for preoperative tumor localization and dosimetry rather than therapeutic intent. The 'BIBH-1' designation suggests potential commercial development, possibly by Boehringer Ingelheim (BI), but this cannot be confirmed from the trial information. F19 is the antibody name. The specific antigen target for F19/BIBH-1 in colorectal cancer is not stated. I-131 is used here as a diagnostic radionuclide (gamma imaging for biodistribution), not for therapy. Radioiodination is the standard labeling method for antibodies with I-131. | WEB ENRICHMENT: Target antigen identified as FAP (Fibroblast Activation Protein) from PMID:8201382, a phase I study describing 'monoclonal antibody F19 (131I-mAbF19)' which 'recognizes the fibroblast activation protein (FAP)'. Confidence HIGH: The paper explicitly describes F19 (also designated BIBH-1) targeting FAP in colorectal cancer metastases. Asset_Owner not disclosed in abstracts (would require full-text author affiliations). | WEB SEARCH ENRICHMENT: Asset owner identified as Boehringer Ingelheim from AdisInsight database which lists co-originator as Boehringer Ingelheim and Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research. Multiple sources confirm Boehringer Ingelheim Pharma KG developed BIBH-1 (sibrotuzumab) for cancer treatment. Patent assignment also confirms Boehringer Ingelheim International as the assignee.

False positive - not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. This trial uses lenvatinib (a small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor) in combination with I-131 radioiodine therapy. The I-131 is standard thyroid ablation therapy using iodide uptake by thyroid cells, not an antibody-radiopharmaceutical conjugate. Lenvatinib and I-131 are administered as separate agents, with lenvatinib potentially enhancing radioiodine sensitivity. No antibody component is present in this study.
Conditions: Differentiated Thyroid Gland Carcinoma, Thyroid Gland Follicular Carcinoma, Thyroid Gland Papillary Carcinoma

Notes: FALSE POSITIVE: This trial uses recombinant human TSH (rhTSH, thyroid stimulating hormone) combined with radioiodine (I-131) for treating multinodular goiter. This is NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. rhTSH is a hormone protein, not an antibody or antibody-like molecule. The radioiodine is administered separately and taken up by thyroid tissue through the sodium-iodide symporter, not delivered via antibody targeting. This is a hormone therapy combined with radioiodine ablation, not RIT.
Countries: Brazil, Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, United States

Notes: False positive. This trial uses I-131 MIBG (metaiodobenzylguanidine), which is a small-molecule radioligand targeting the norepinephrine transporter, NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. MIBG is structurally similar to norepinephrine and is taken up by neuroblastoma cells via active transport. While rituximab (an antibody) is listed in interventions, it is used as an unconjugated antibody for B-cell depletion as part of the conditioning regimen, not as a radiolabeled conjugate. The trial combines targeted radionuclide therapy (MIBG) with haploidentical stem cell transplantation and immunotherapy, but does not involve RIT (antibody-based radiopharmaceuticals).

Asset_Owner: Garden State Cancer Center at the Center for Molecular Medicine and Immunology
Notes: This is a pretargeted imaging study using a bispecific antibody (anti-CEA x anti-HSG). TF2 is a bispecific antibody that binds both CEA (tumor target) and HSG (hapten-peptide). In pretargeting approaches, the bispecific antibody is administered first, followed by a radiolabeled hapten. However, in this trial the antibody itself is labeled with I-131 for biodistribution and pharmacokinetic assessment. The study focuses on preoperative detection/imaging (scintigraphy), making this purely diagnostic. Purpose states 'find colorectal cancer cells and learn the extent of disease' - diagnostic intent. Garden State Cancer Center/CMMI developed the TF2 bispecific antibody platform.
Lead_Sponsor: Garden State Cancer Center at the Center for Molecular Medicine and Immunology
Asset_Owner_Norm: Garden State Cancer Center at the Center for Molecular Medicine and Immunology

Notes: This is a combination trial testing 131I-huA33 (RIT component) with capecitabine chemotherapy. The antibody huA33 is a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting the A33 antigen expressed on colorectal cancer cells. Radioiodine (131I) is directly conjugated to the antibody. The trial involves dose escalation of both the radioactivity (20-40 mCi/m2 131I) and capecitabine (1000-1500 mg/m2/day). Only one treatment infusion of 131I-huA33 is given per patient, followed by 4 cycles of capecitabine. The A33 antigen is a validated target for colorectal cancer. This is a classic therapeutic RIT approach using direct radioiodination of a humanized IgG antibody. Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research is the lead sponsor and appears to be the asset owner for huA33.

False positive: 131I-MIBG (meta-iodobenzylguanidine) is a small-molecule radioligand that targets the norepinephrine transporter, not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. This trial combines 131I-MIBG with the anti-GD2 antibody dinutuximab and/or vorinostat (HDAC inhibitor), but the antibody is NOT radiolabeled - they are separate therapeutic agents administered in combination. Dinutuximab is given IV on Days 8-11 and 29-32, while 131I-MIBG is administered on Day 1, confirming they are not conjugated. This is a combination therapy study, not an RIT trial.
Lead_Sponsor: New Approaches to Neuroblastoma Therapy Consortium

Notes: Licartin is a well-characterized Chinese-developed RIT product for hepatocellular carcinoma. Metuximab targets CD147 (HAb18G/CD147), a surface antigen overexpressed in liver cancer. I-131 is directly labeled to the antibody (typical for radioiodine labeling). Trial tests combination therapy: Licartin + TACE (transcatheter arterial chemoembolization). This is China's first approved RIT drug with domestic intellectual property rights. Antibody format inferred as full-length IgG based on typical murine/chimeric antibody structure for Licartin. Asset owner likely Chengdu Huasun Technology, which commercialized Licartin, though trial sponsor is academic (Eastern Hepatobiliary Surgery Hospital).

Cotara (chTNT-1/B) is a chimeric antibody that targets necrotic tissue by binding to DNA-histone complexes exposed in tumor necrosis. The antibody is directly labeled with I-131. Unique delivery method: interstitial infusion directly into tumor mass over 25 hours at 2.5 mCi/cc. TNT stands for Tumor Necrosis Therapy. This is a well-characterized RIT asset developed by Peregrine Pharmaceuticals specifically for solid tumors with necrotic cores, particularly GBM.

False positive. This trial uses radioactive iodine (I-131) for thyroid ablation in Graves' disease, not as a radiopharmaceutical conjugated to an antibody. The study compares I-131 thyroid treatment vs. carbimazole (anti-thyroid drug) to assess risk of ophthalmopathy development. Although the study mentions 'orbital antibodies' in the title/aims, these are endogenous autoantibodies being measured as biomarkers, not therapeutic antibody-based radiopharmaceuticals. This is standard nuclear medicine thyroid therapy, not RIT.

False positive. 131-I-mIBG (metaiodobenzylguanidine) is a small-molecule radioligand, not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. mIBG is a norepinephrine analog that targets norepinephrine transporter (NET) in neuroblastoma cells. The trial combines this small-molecule radiopharmaceutical with two separate antibodies: Nivolumab (anti-PD1 checkpoint inhibitor) and ch14.18/CHO/Dinutuximab Beta (anti-GD2 antibody), but neither antibody is radiolabeled. This is a combination therapy of a small-molecule radiopharmaceutical with unconjugated immunotherapies, not RIT.
Lead_Sponsor: University Hospital Southampton NHS Foundation Trust

Notes: This is NOT a radioimmunotherapy trial. This is a standard radioiodine (I-131) ablation therapy trial for differentiated thyroid cancer comparing low-dose (30 mCi) versus high-dose (100 mCi) treatment. Radioiodine ablation in thyroid cancer works by direct uptake of iodine by thyroid tissue via the sodium-iodide symporter - no antibody component is involved. This is a common false positive pattern where radioiodine therapy appears in search results but is not antibody-based. The intervention description confirms this is simply radioiodine dosing without any antibody conjugate.

False positive. This is an observational/diagnostic study measuring serum anti-p53 antibody levels as a biomarker for dedifferentiation in thyroid cancer patients. The I-131 mentioned refers to conventional radioiodine scanning for thyroid cancer follow-up (standard of care), not a radioimmunoconjugate. No antibody-based radiopharmaceutical is being tested or administered. The 'p53 antibody' refers to autoantibodies against p53 protein detected in patient serum, not a therapeutic or diagnostic radiopharmaceutical.

Alpha particle radioimmunotherapy using Pb-212, an alpha-emitter with short path length. TCMC (1,4,7,10-tetraaza-1,4,7,10-tetra-(2-carbamoyl methyl)-cyclododecane) is a macrocyclic chelator suitable for Pb-212. Trastuzumab is a well-established humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting HER2. Administered intraperitoneally for intraperitoneal cancers. Orano Med (formerly Areva Med) specializes in alpha-emitting radiopharmaceuticals. Phase I dose escalation starting at 200 μCi/m². Cold trastuzumab (4 mg/kg) also administered, likely to saturate HER2 receptors and improve targeting specificity.
Conditions: Breast Neoplasms, Peritoneal Neoplasms, Ovarian Neoplasms, Pancreatic Neoplasms, Stomach Neoplasms

Notes: Alpha-emitting RIT using astatine-211 targeting CD38 for multiple myeloma. OKT10-B10 is an anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody. At-211 is typically directly labeled to antibodies (halogenation) rather than using a metal chelator. This is part of a conditioning regimen before allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation. The trial is sponsored by Fred Hutch, suggesting in-house development. CD38 is highly expressed on multiple myeloma cells and is a validated target (daratumumab). This represents an alpha-particle emitter RIT approach, which is less common than beta-emitters but potentially more potent for targeted cell killing.
Conditions: Multiple Myeloma, Recurrent Multiple Myeloma, Refractory Multiple Myeloma

Notes: This is a well-characterized RIT asset from Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center. BC8 is an established anti-CD45 murine IgG1 monoclonal antibody, and BC8-B10 appears to be a variant or production designation. At-211 is an alpha-emitting radioisotope that requires direct radiohalogenation (similar to radioiodination) rather than chelation, as astatine is a halogen. The trial uses RIT as part of reduced-intensity conditioning for hematopoietic cell transplantation in nonmalignant diseases. CD45 is expressed on all hematopoietic cells, making it an appropriate target for myeloablative conditioning. This represents therapeutic use of targeted alpha therapy combined with external beam TBI and chemotherapy conditioning.

This is a diagnostic PET imaging study using Zr-89-labeled olaratumab to image PDGFRα expression in soft tissue sarcoma. The trial explicitly states the goal is to provide proof-of-concept tumor targeting and assess dosimetry. DFOsq (deferoxamine-squaramide) is a modified DFO chelator commonly used for Zr-89. The description mentions this will inform future development of olaratumab as a therapeutic radiopharmaceutical, suggesting potential theranostic strategy with paired therapeutic isotope (e.g., Lu-177 or Ac-225) in future. Olaratumab is a human IgG1 monoclonal antibody. PDGFRα (Platelet-Derived Growth Factor Receptor alpha) is the validated target. This is a Phase 1 first-in-human imaging trial focused on safety, dosimetry, pharmacokinetics and imaging properties.

FALSE POSITIVE: This is a pharmacodynamic study of tocilizumab (unconjugated anti-IL-6R antibody) in healthy volunteers. No radioisotope is mentioned. This is not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. Tocilizumab/RoActemra/Actemra is used to study neutrophil distribution kinetics via standard pharmacodynamic methods, not radioimmunotherapy. This trial was likely matched due to 'kinetics' keyword overlap with radiotracer biodistribution studies.

False positive. BMN 111 (vosoritide) is a C-type natriuretic peptide analogue for treating achondroplasia (dwarfism). This is a small peptide drug, not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. No radiolabeling is involved. The trial is for a skeletal/bone growth disorder, not cancer or a condition treated with radioimmunotherapy.
Countries: Australia, Japan, United Kingdom, United States

Notes: This is a dual-modality imaging agent combining Indium-111 (for SPECT/CT and intraoperative radiodetection) with IRDye800CW (for near-infrared fluorescence imaging). The primary purpose is intraoperative imaging guidance during nephrectomy, not therapeutic. Girentuximab is a well-characterized chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting CAIX, which is expressed in >95% of clear cell renal cell carcinomas. The dual-labeling approach allows both preoperative SPECT/CT imaging and intraoperative fluorescence/radiodetection guidance. Asset owner is listed as academic sponsor Radboud University Medical Center, though girentuximab was originally developed by WILEX (later Telix Pharmaceuticals). This is purely diagnostic imaging - no therapeutic radioisotope involved.

Novel VHH-Fc format targeting DLL3 in SCLC/LCNEC. Keywords explicitly mention 'VHH-Fc' and 'HH-FcRN' format. Theranostic pair: Ac-225 for therapy and In-111 (111In-ABD147) for diagnostic imaging/dosimetry. Specific linker-chelator mentioned but proprietary composition not disclosed. DLL3 is a validated neuroendocrine tumor target. First-in-human study. VHH-Fc combines nanobody (VHH) targeting domain with Fc region for extended half-life via FcRn binding.
Conditions: Small-Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC), Large Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma of the Lung

Notes: This is a Phase I biodistribution and imaging study using In-111 labeled ch806 antibody. Ch806 is a chimeric (mouse-human) monoclonal antibody targeting a specific epitope of EGFR (the 806 antigen) that is preferentially expressed on tumor cells with EGFR mutation/overexpression. The trial uses trace amounts of In-111 for gamma camera imaging to assess biodistribution, pharmacokinetics, and tumor uptake. This is purely diagnostic/imaging - not therapeutic. The specific chelator used to attach In-111 is not mentioned but is likely DTPA or DOTA based on standard In-111 labeling methods. Single dose only, no therapeutic intent. | WEB ENRICHMENT: PMID:15770208 explicitly states ch806 was radiolabelled with indium-111, demonstrating successful radiolabeling. PMID:27457521 directly compares (111)In-CHX-A''-DTPA-ch806 as a reference standard, confirming CHX-A''-DTPA as the chelator used for indium-111 labeling of ch806. Both papers clearly link to the same antibody (ch806) targeting the 806 epitope of EGFR, matching the trial asset.

This is a dual-modality imaging agent combining Indium-111 (for SPECT imaging and radiodetection) with IRDye800CW (for near-infrared fluorescence imaging). Labetuzumab is a humanized anti-CEA antibody. The compound is used for intraoperative imaging guidance during cytoreductive surgery, not for therapy. CEA is expressed on >95% of colorectal cancers. The dual-labeling approach enables preoperative SPECT/CT imaging (day 4-5) and intraoperative guidance with both gamma probe and fluorescence camera (day 7). This is purely diagnostic/imaging use, not radioimmunotherapy. Academic-sponsored study at Radboud; labetuzumab was originally developed by Immunomedics (now part of Gilead).
Conditions: Peritoneal Carcinomatosis, Colorectal Cancer, Carcinoma, Neoplasms, Gastrointestinal Cancer

Asset_Owner: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS)
Notes: This is a diagnostic immunoPET imaging study using an 80 kDa anti-CD8 minibody radiolabeled with Zr-89 (T1/2 78.4 hours) for PET imaging. The minibody is explicitly described as having high affinity to CD8 glycoprotein (EC50 = 0.4 nM) and is conjugated with deferoxamine (Df). The study aims to visualize CD8+ T cell infiltration in CNS demyelinating diseases (MS and PML). This is purely diagnostic/imaging - no therapeutic intent. Minibodies are engineered antibody fragments (~80 kDa, composed of scFv linked to CH3 domain) that offer faster clearance than full IgG, making them ideal for Zr-89 imaging. Asset owner listed as NINDS (sponsor), though commercial rights may exist elsewhere.
Lead_Sponsor: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS)
Asset_Owner_Norm: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS)

Notes: This is an immunoPET imaging agent designed to visualize CD8+ T cells. The minibody format (crefmirlimab) is explicitly stated. DFO (deferoxamine, also called 'berdoxam' in the nomenclature) is the standard chelator for Zr-89. The purpose is purely diagnostic - to assess immune response and T cell distribution during TIL therapy, not therapeutic intent. The intervention description clearly states it is a PET/CT imaging study with the radiolabeled minibody as the investigational agent.
Conditions: Lung Cancer, Non Small Cell Lung Cancer, Metastatic Non Small Cell Lung Cancer

Notes: False positive: This trial uses PET-CT (Positron Emission Tomography) as a diagnostic imaging tool to guide treatment decisions, not as a therapeutic intervention. The treatment is R-CHOP chemotherapy (rituximab + chemotherapy) with potential radiation therapy. Rituximab is an unconjugated monoclonal antibody, not a radioimmunoconjugate. PET-CT is used for response assessment (Deauville scoring) to adapt treatment strategy. No antibody-based radiopharmaceutical is involved.
Lead_Sponsor: Grupo Argentino de Tratamiento de la Leucemia Aguda

Notes: False positive: This trial tests premedication strategies (cetirizine vs diphenhydramine) to prevent hypersensitivity reactions during chemotherapy infusions. While rituximab (anti-CD20 antibody) is mentioned, it is NOT radiolabeled. The study is about managing infusion reactions, not radioimmunotherapy. No radioisotope is involved. This is a supportive care/premedication study in oncology, not an RIT trial.

This is an observational registry study tracking treatment patterns for lymphatic malignancies in Germany. While ibritumomab-tiuxetan (Zevalin, Y-90 RIT) is mentioned in the keywords as one of many possible therapies being observed, this is NOT an interventional RIT trial. It is an epidemiological registry that will document whatever therapies physicians choose to use, including but not limited to rituximab, bendamustine, lenalidomide, bortezomib, and potentially ibritumomab-tiuxetan. No specific intervention is being administered as part of the study protocol. Classification: Not an RIT trial - observational registry only.

Notes: This is a diagnostic PET imaging study using 68Ga-BMS986192 to visualize PD-L1 expression in NSCLC patients receiving immunotherapy. The tracer BMS986192 is likely a small molecule or peptide-based PD-L1 ligand, not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. The description mentions it was synthesized and evaluated preclinically but does not specify antibody format. Given the naming convention (BMS986192 suggests Bristol Myers Squibb compound) and the focus on small-scale 68Ga labeling technology, this appears to be a small-molecule radioligand rather than a radiolabeled antibody. Classification: Diagnostic imaging agent, but likely NOT an antibody-based RIT. The study aims to assess PD-L1 expression heterogeneity and predict response to checkpoint inhibitors. | WEB ENRICHMENT: Article explicitly describes 68Ga-BMS-986192 as a DOTA-conjugated adnectin protein targeting PD-L1. The chelator is clearly stated as DOTA (DOTA-conjugated adnectin, BXA-206362). The protein format is identified as an adnectin, which is a distinct antibody alternative scaffold. Asset owner inferred as Bristol Myers Squibb from the BMS prefix in the compound name (BMS-986192, BXA-206362). Direct conjugation is the linker technology for DOTA attachment to the adnectin scaffold.

False positive - not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. TLX101-Tx (Iodofalan I-131) is a radiolabeled amino acid analog that targets the LAT-1 (L-type amino acid transporter 1) system. This is a small molecule radioligand, not an antibody-based therapeutic. The compound uses the natural amino acid transport mechanism for tumor targeting, similar to how amino acids are taken up by cells. Keywords mention 'LAT-1 targeted therapy' which confirms this is amino acid-based targeting, not antibody-based. This is analogous to other small molecule radiopharmaceuticals like PSMA-617 or DOTATATE that use peptide/small molecule targeting rather than antibody scaffolds.
Conditions: Neoplastic Disease, Glioblastoma, Glioblastoma (GBM), Glioblastoma Multiform, Glioblastoma Multiforme, Adult, Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM) WHO Grade IV

Notes: False positive: 18F-Floretyrosine (18F-FET) is a radiolabeled amino acid analog, not an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. This is NOT an antibody-based radiopharmaceutical. TLX090-Tx (153Sm-DOTMP) is a small-molecule bone-seeking radiopharmaceutical. DOTMP (tetramethylene phosphonate) is a bisphosphonate-like molecule that targets bone mineral, not an antibody targeting a tumor antigen. This is a classic false positive - it uses a radioisotope (Sm-153) and has the word 'radioligand' in keywords, but the ligand is a small phosphonate molecule, not an antibody or antibody-like protein scaffold. Similar agents include Sm-153 lexidronam (Quadramet) and Sr-89 chloride. Asset owner is Telix Pharmaceuticals based on sponsor information.

No antibody component is involved. This is a diagnostic imaging study using PET, not radioimmunotherapy.

This is a diagnostic imaging trial using Zr-89-labeled girentuximab for PET/CT imaging of CAIX in VHL disease. Girentuximab is a chimeric IgG1 antibody targeting carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX). The study explicitly mentions theranostic potential - they plan to simulate therapeutic dosimetry by modeling replacement of Zr-89 with therapeutic isotopes. This makes it a theranostic exploratory study. DFO (desferrioxamine) is the standard chelator for Zr-89. Asset_Owner is likely the academic sponsor, though girentuximab was originally developed by Wilex/Telix Pharmaceuticals. Category set to Theranostic based on stated theranostic simulation objectives, though current phase is purely diagnostic imaging.

Novel alpha-emitter RIT for bladder cancer via intravesical instillation. Girentuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting CAIX. At-211 is an alpha emitter with short path length ideal for superficial targets. Prior PET/CT feasibility study used [89Zr]Zr-girentuximab for imaging validation (NCT04897763). Theranostic pair: Zr-89 (diagnostic) and At-211 (therapeutic), though this trial uses only therapeutic isotope. At-211 is typically directly labeled to antibodies (radiohalogenation), not via metal chelator. FIH study for intravesical administration in BCG-unresponsive NMIBC. CAIX overexpressed in 70-90% of NMIBC cases. Asset_Owner likely ICO as academic developer; commercial rights unclear.

This is a diagnostic PET/CT imaging study using 89Zr-girentuximab to detect CAIX expression in non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer. Girentuximab is a well-characterized chimeric IgG1 antibody targeting carbonic anhydrase IX. The radiopharmaceutical is administered via intravesical instillation (directly into the bladder), which is an unusual route for antibody-based imaging but appropriate for local bladder cancer detection. ATONCO is listed as a collaborator and likely owns or licenses TLX250 technology. DFO is the standard chelator for Zr-89 labeling of antibodies. This is purely diagnostic imaging, not therapeutic RIT. CAIX is expressed in 70-90% of bladder cancers per the trial description.

This is a diagnostic imaging trial using 111In-girentuximab for SPECT imaging to evaluate treatment response after cryoablation of renal cell carcinoma. Girentuximab is a chimeric monoclonal antibody targeting carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX), which is overexpressed in clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The radiolabeled antibody is used for imaging only, not therapy. This agent was commercialized as TLX250-CDx (Zirconium-89 version) and Redectane (In-111 version) by Telix Pharmaceuticals. The trial is evaluating whether post-ablation SPECT imaging can detect residual viable tumor cells that express CAIX.

This trial is NOT a RIT study. The primary therapeutic intervention is sunitinib malate (a small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor), not a radiolabeled antibody. The I-124 girentuximab (chimeric monoclonal antibody G250) mentioned in interventions is used solely as a diagnostic imaging agent for PET imaging to assess tumor vascular permeability - it is not the therapeutic agent. The study aims to correlate imaging and histologic measures with clinical outcomes in patients receiving sunitinib therapy before and after cytoreductive nephrectomy. This is a false positive match - the radiolabeled antibody is used for imaging/biomarker assessment, not as the therapeutic intervention.

Affibody molecules are small (~7 kDa) engineered protein scaffolds derived from Staphylococcus aureus protein A. They are distinct from antibodies but function as antibody alternatives with high-affinity binding. This is a first-in-human trial evaluating biodistribution and safety of a Lu-177-labeled Affibody targeting HER2 in metastatic breast cancer. The trial design (biodistribution study with dose optimization) is typical for therapeutic radiopharmaceutical development. Chelator is likely DOTA or similar bifunctional chelator commonly used for Lu-177, but not explicitly stated. Affibody's naming convention uses ABY- prefix for their molecules.
Conditions: Metastatic Breast Cancer, HER2 + Breast Cancer
